entities.label: University of Michigan CME digital text CME00049
notes: The encoding includes Brunner's title page, part title, Einrichtung der Ausgabe on printed pp. 79–80, the main critical text on pp. 81–450, and the alternative W/A ending on pp. 450–452.
It omits Brunner's introduction on pp. 1–78, post-text annotations on pp. 453–459, word list on pp. 460–461, names index on pp. 462–474, and German translation on pp. 475–604.

bibliographic.citation: Karl Brunner, ed., Der mittelenglische Versroman über Richard Löwenherz: kritische Ausgabe nach allen Handschriften mit Einleitung, Anmerkungen und deutscher Übersetzung, Wiener Beiträge zur englischen Philologie 42 (Wien and Leipzig: Wilhelm Braumüller, 1913).
bibliographic.title: Der mittelenglische Versroman über Richard Löwenherz

entities.label: Karl Brunner's Der mittelenglische Versroman über Richard Löwenherz (1913)
notes: The critical text occupies pp. 81–452; Brunner's main reading is the a-version, based principally on C and supplemented from W1 where C is defective.
Divergent b-version passages appear below the main text in italics, based on A where possible; purely orthographic variants were normally omitted.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1913, and the U-M item made from it is explicitly marked No Copyright–United States.
rights.component: 1913 scholarly edition text

next_steps: Request whole-object quotations from each holding institution, explicitly including all text leaves, blanks, endleaves, pastedowns, binding exteriors, spine, edges, mounts, enclosures, and color targets where feasible.
For BL holdings, submit separate requests for Add MS 31042, Egerton MS 2862, and Harley MS 4690 through the current digitisation service.
Record any returned terms, exact scope, identifiers, omitted surfaces, and publication restrictions without treating a work-only range as a whole manuscript.
openQuestions.question: Can complete whole-object facsimiles be obtained for C, B, E, H, A, the Badminton fragment, and the 1509 and 1528 print copies?

next_steps: Request the five later front leaves, one later rear leaf, boards, spine, binding exteriors, pastedowns, edges, and any other uncaptured surfaces.
If supplied, cache the complete received object separately and retain the public 80-canvas bundle's narrower coverage statement.
openQuestions.question: Will the Bodleian supply images of the physical surfaces omitted from the 80-canvas Douce 228 inventory?

next_steps: Ask each current holder separately for all physical surfaces of its object, not only Richard leaves.
Preserve the reconstructed textual order separately from the provider inventory order and physical custody order.
Seek explicit permission before publishing any supplied images.
openQuestions.question: Can the NLS, Edinburgh, and St Andrews supply complete physical-object photography beyond the legacy Auchinleck project's 701-image inventory?

next_steps: Consult current stemmatic and multitext scholarship and compare complete witnesses directly.
Keep Brunner's a/b classification scoped to his editorial method and avoid asserting direct copying among surviving witnesses.
Treat the St Andrews and Badminton fragments as modern supporting witnesses rather than unacknowledged inputs to Brunner.
openQuestions.question: How should the a- and b-version witnesses, early prints, and later-found fragments be related without imposing a false linear stemma?

reviewNotes: The intellectual subject is the anonymous Middle English verse romance or crusading poem Richard Coer de Lyon, not Richard I as a historical person, any single witness codex, Brunner's critical edition, the U-M encoding, or a facsimile.

The textual tradition is fluid. Brunner groups C, B, W1, and W2 as version a and A, H, L, E, and D as version b; later scholarship treats those groupings as useful but does not establish that one surviving witness is copied directly from another.

The XML contains 8,064 L elements: 7,268 direct reading-text lines and 796 apparatus-nested witness readings. Its 7,212-line main reading is followed by a 56-line alternative ending that replaces the passage beginning at Brunner line 7203 rather than extending the work to 7,268 sequential numbered lines.

Whole-manuscript caching follows the complete provider object where practical. The 80-image Douce bundle covers every published medieval folio face, and the 701-image Auchinleck bundle covers the complete legacy project inventory; both are marked partial at physical-object level and explicitly identify surfaces their providers did not publish.

rights.basis: This is a modern scholarly edition; public reading and download were verified but no license authorizing redistribution was found.
rights.component: 2015 editorial text, introduction, notes, and downloadable PDF

lineage.summary: The checked-in CME00049 XML represents the University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse text keyed from Karl Brunner's 1913 critical edition of Richard Coer de Lyon. Brunner constructed his continuous a-version reading chiefly from Gonville and Caius College MS 175/96 (C), supplied its gaps from Wynkyn de Worde's 1509 print (W1), and selectively printed divergent b-version passages from College of Arms MS HDN 58 (A) while collating the other witnesses and the 1528 print. The XML preserves Brunner's edited text, extensive apparatus, and an alternative W/A ending, but not the entire 604-page edition. A complete scan of Brunner, complete public digital inventories for Bodleian MS Douce 228 and the dispersed Auchinleck witness, a complete EEBO-TCP transcription of the 1509 print, and complete modern supporting editions are cached. The manuscript bundles exhaust the public provider inventories found but remain partial at physical-object level because their providers omit surfaces.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Probably composed circa 1300; first securely attested in the Auchinleck Manuscript in the 1330s; first surviving print dated 1509; immediate critical edition published in 1913
catalogSummary.region: Middle English work of English cultural origin; London is a tentative rather than secure composition localization
catalogSummary.title: Richard Coer de Lyon

catalogingSubject.label: Richard Coer de Lyon
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the anonymous Middle English verse romance and crusading poem conventionally called Richard Coer de Lyon, represented by a fluid tradition with materially different a and b versions. CME00049 presents Karl Brunner's C-based a-version critical reading, extensive witness apparatus, and a 56-line W/A alternative ending. The work record excludes Richard I as a historical person, any presumed lost Anglo-Norman source, the individual manuscript codices and early printed books, Brunner's edition and German translation, the U-M encoding, the repository XML, and facsimiles as distinct textual, editorial, digital, and physical layers.

contentParts.label: Brunner's main a-version critical reading
notes: The dense apparatus is editorial metadata around this reading and is not added to its 7,212-line extent.
contentParts.summary: A continuous 7,212-line reading principally based on Gonville and Caius MS 175/96 (C), with gaps supplied from Wynkyn de Worde's 1509 print (W1), covering Richard's legendary origins, chivalric and crusading career, death, and prayer for his soul.

contentParts.label: Alternative W/A ending

locators.value: pp. 450–452; replacement beginning at main line 7203

notes: This is a mutually alternative ending, not an extra epilogue meant to follow line 7212.
contentParts.summary: A 56-line alternative ending printed by Brunner from W and A, beginning as a replacement for the main reading at line 7203 and closing with an Amen request.

assertion.notes: The normalized range is an operational expression of circa 1300, not a dated authorial event. Figueredo's more specific historical reconstruction is a scholarly hypothesis rather than the preferred consensus date.
This dates the earliest securely dated surviving witness, not composition. Its Richard leaves are now split among the NLS, Edinburgh, and St Andrews.

dateStatements.display: Attested by the dispersed Auchinleck witness, produced in London in the 1330s

assertion.notes: This identifies the first surviving early print represented in the researched lineage; it does not prove that no lost printing preceded it.

dateStatements.display: Wynkyn de Worde printed a surviving edition in London in 1509
Brunner's immediate critical source edition was published in 1913

extent.description: The XML has 8,064 L elements in total. Exactly 7,268 are direct reading-text lines: 7,212 in Brunner's main a-version reading and 56 in a separately printed alternative W/A ending. The remaining 796 L elements are nested in apparatus notes and are witness variants or divergent passages. Because the 56-line ending replaces the main text beginning at Brunner line 7203, 7,268 is an encoded direct-line count across mutually alternative parts, not a claim that the work has 7,268 sequential canonical lines. The XML also has 5 DIV1, 1 DIV2, 377 PB, and 8,167 NOTE elements.

formStatements.description: Narrative verse in rhyming couplets; Brunner's prose method and apparatus are editorial layers rather than the work's textual form.

assertion.notes: The poem freely reshapes the historical Richard I and Third Crusade with legendary, marvelous, and polemical episodes.
Brief Latin rubrication and German editorial matter belong to embedded or edition layers and do not make the medieval work itself a mixed-language composition.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: Latin is omitted from the compact language-code summary because it is limited to a rubric rather than substantive work text.
The prose/verse classification applies to the medieval work: verse. Brunner's German editorial introduction, apparatus prose, and translation remain source-edition layers.

The work contains extreme anti-Muslim polemic, religious violence, dehumanization, and cannibalism. Neutral cataloging should make those features discoverable and contextualized rather than euphemizing or reproducing them as endorsement.

The linked lineage caches complete source objects where publicly practical, then records the exact work-bearing folios/images in work_portion. A complete published inventory is not mislabeled as a complete physical-object surrogate when the provider omits surfaces.

The medieval work and Brunner's 1913 edition are public domain in the United States through the recorded U-M rights statement, but Bodleian, NLS, White Rose, and METS image or modern-edition layers have separate permissions and must not be redistributed merely because they are publicly downloadable.

next_steps: Review current linguistic, historical, and textual scholarship across both major versions.
Test Figueredo's post-1300 historical reconstruction against competing dating arguments and external allusions.
Retain London at low confidence until work-level evidence goes beyond the London production of Auchinleck or de Worde's print.
openQuestions.question: Can the circa-1300 composition date and tentative London localization be narrowed without confusing work origin with the date, dialect, or production place of individual witnesses?

next_steps: Compare the Middle English versions directly with extant Anglo-Norman and Latin Third Crusade narratives.
Separate internal source claims, shared historical material, and demonstrable verbal dependence.
Do not create a confirmed lost-source entity or translation relationship without stronger evidence.
openQuestions.question: Did Richard Coer de Lyon depend on a now-lost Anglo-Norman work, or are its internal French-source claims literary framing rather than evidence of a recoverable exemplar?

next_steps: Collate complete witnesses rather than inheriting Brunner's editorial categories uncritically.
Review Finlayson's distinct-work argument and current multitext modeling.
Keep CME's main reading and alternative ending as explicit content parts without presenting Brunner's critical reconstruction as a diplomatic witness.
openQuestions.question: Should the a and b textual groupings be cataloged as versions of one work, distinct works, or a more fluid multitext tradition?

next_steps: Preserve separate counts for direct main reading, direct alternative ending, and NOTE-nested apparatus lines.
Map every alternative-ending line to the corresponding replaced main passage and source siglum.
Display version-aware extent in the web viewer rather than a single unexplained total.
openQuestions.question: How should main-text lineation, the 56-line replacement ending, and 796 apparatus-nested witness lines be exposed to users without implying one additive canonical extent?

assertion.notes: The specialist romance database marks London with a question mark. Auchinleck's London production and de Worde's London printing do not independently prove the work was composed there.

scope.description: opening and closing English episodes and Richard's kingship
the crusading narrative around Acre and the Levant

assertion.notes: No named medieval author is established. Internal claims about French source material do not identify an author.

scope.description: CME's edition-level presentation of the medieval work
critical text, apparatus, b-version divergences, and alternative ending

subjects.label: Richard I, King of England
notes: The literary protagonist is based on the historical king but includes legendary biography; the work is not a reliable documentary chronicle.

scope.description: notorious a-version episode involving Saracen captives

assertion.notes: The poem repeatedly dehumanizes Muslims and celebrates extreme crusading violence. This subject label records content rather than endorsing it; the RCLM project notes later racist appropriation.
Brunner's a/b categories are editorially useful, but surviving witnesses should not be forced into a simple direct-copy chain.

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English romance recasts Richard I's life and the Third Crusade as heroic, marvelous, and violently polemical narrative. In the expansive a version represented by Brunner's main reading, Richard's demon-mother legend, feats of strength, crusading battles, sieges, and notorious cannibalism episode build an idealized English warrior king while dehumanizing Muslim opponents. The materially different b tradition omits or reshapes important episodes, and the surviving witnesses resist reduction to a single linear form.

CME00049 encodes Brunner's 7,212-line main a-version reading and a separately printed 56-line W/A ending that replaces the main passage beginning at line 7203. It also carries 8,167 apparatus notes containing 796 additional L elements for variants and divergent witness readings. Those nested lines and the mutually exclusive ending are not a sequential extension of the work's main reading.

The metadata describes the medieval work and its CME textual presentation separately from Brunner's German editorial prose and translation. The linked lineage records C as Brunner's continuous base, W1 as the principal source for C's gaps, A as the preferred b-version source where available, and the remaining manuscripts and W2 as collated witnesses. Later-found St Andrews and Badminton fragments support study of the tradition but were not inputs to the 1913 edition.

Work, textual version, physical witness, historical print, critical edition, digital encoding, repository artifact, and facsimile remain separate layers. Manuscript production dates and dialects do not become composition facts; a provider's complete image inventory does not become a complete physical-object facsimile when binding surfaces or later leaves are absent; and public download does not imply permission to redistribute modern images or editions.

titles.supplied_by: modern scholarship and the immediate edition

assertion.notes: The Latin rubric is an edition/witness title signal, not demonstrated authorial wording.

titles.value: Hic incipit uita Ricardi regis primi

assertion.notes: This is Brunner's C/W/B-supported critical incipit, not a claim that every witness begins identically or survives at its beginning.
Brunner numbers the final two main-reading lines 7211–7212 and says they are absent from B.

titles.value: And oure soules whenne we come þertoo!

assertion.notes: This closes Brunner's separately printed 56-line W/A ending that replaces the main text from line 7203 onward.

work.summary: An anonymous Middle English romance recasts Richard I's life and the Third Crusade as heroic, marvelous, and violently polemical narrative. In the expansive a version represented by Brunner's main reading, Richard's demon-mother legend, feats of strength, crusading battles, sieges, and notorious cannibalism episode build an idealized English warrior king while dehumanizing Muslim opponents. The materially different b tradition omits or reshapes important episodes, and the surviving witnesses resist reduction to a single linear form.
